title: The Texas Screwworm Cases Are a Wake-Up Call for U.S. Biosecurity
gist: A RAND biosecurity researcher argues the 2026 New World screwworm reemergence in Texas exposes how poorly the U.S. defends its concentrated crop base against naturally occurring or deliberately engineered pathogens, and proposes leveraging existing surveillance, diagnostic, and forensic infrastructure rather than building new bureaucracy.

insights.claim: The New World screwworm, a flesh-eating parasite eradicated from the U.S. roughly 60 years ago, was reconfirmed in U.S. livestock on June 3, 2026, with 15 confirmed cases across Texas and southeastern New Mexico within three weeks.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: A pest eradicated for two generations is back despite preparation; watch USDA emergency-response spending and cattle/beef supply-chain effects in the affected region.
insights.quote: Within three weeks, 15 cases in livestock and pets have been confirmed across Texas and southeastern New Mexico.
insights.locator: The U.S. Department of Agriculture confirmed June 3

insights.claim: Even with USDA modeling that predicted the fly's return plus a coordinated response of quarantine zones, trapping, sterile-fly releases, and emergency drug approvals, U.S. authorities could not prevent screwworm's reemergence.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: If the best-rehearsed agricultural threat still broke through, less-anticipated threats are likely to as well; treat current preparedness as a floor, not a ceiling.
insights.quote: Still, we were unable to prevent its reemergence in the United States. And screwworm is the threat we rehearsed for.
insights.locator: Although there is plenty to criticize

insights.claim: The U.S. Corn Belt produces more than one-third of all U.S. corn and 34 percent of the world's soybeans, with combined export value exceeding $34 billion, making it a high-value single point of failure.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: A concentrated, high-value export base means a successful pathogen introduction could move global grain and oilseed prices; relevant to agricultural commodity exposure.
insights.quote: Their combined export value exceeds $34 billion.
insights.locator: The Corn Belt produces more than one-third

insights.claim: Planting tens of millions of acres with a small number of high-yield crop varieties in rotation makes U.S. agriculture efficient but genetically fragile and vulnerable to a single matched pathogen.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Monoculture concentration is a systemic risk factor; genetic-diversity and seed-resilience plays are the structural hedge.
insights.quote: This kind of farming is efficient but also fragile.

insights.claim: When a single genetic corn strain dominated U.S. farming in the 1970s, Southern corn leaf blight destroyed 15 percent of the North American crop, and crop-strain concentration is by some measures more pronounced today.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: A documented historical precedent shows double-digit crop losses are possible from one pathogen; current concentration raises, not lowers, that exposure.
insights.quote: an outbreak of Southern corn leaf blight destroyed 15 percent (PDF) of the North American crop
insights.locator: In the 1970s, when a single genetic strain

insights.claim: Agricultural sabotage requires little sophistication: cyanide-laced Chilean grapes in 1989 triggered an import suspension costing Chile's agriculture sector $330 million.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Low-cost contamination can inflict nine-figure trade losses; tail risk to any country's agricultural export franchise.
insights.quote: the import suspension that followed cost Chile's agriculture sector $330 million
insights.locator: In 1989, terrorists laced a few grapes

insights.claim: A 1978 mercury-injection campaign against Israeli oranges exported to Europe cut European orange imports from Israel by 40 percent.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that perceived contamination, not just actual yield loss, can collapse export demand; reputational contagion is a real channel.
insights.quote: European orange imports from Israel fell by 40 percent (PDF).

insights.claim: Advances in synthetic biology make it feasible to engineer novel crop-killing pathogens that, released covertly during a growing season, could go undetected for weeks and spread beyond any containable perimeter.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Bioengineering lowers the barrier to catastrophic agricultural attack; a structural risk to food-security and a driver of biosurveillance demand.
insights.quote: Released covertly during a growing season, it could go undetected for weeks—long enough to spread beyond any containable perimeter.

insights.claim: The U.S. monitors wastewater for human pathogens like COVID-19 via the CDC, but screening for new crop pathogens through the National Plant Diagnostic Network is minimal and there is no crop-equivalent of the screwworm response playbook.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: The capability asymmetry between human and crop pathogen surveillance is the exploitable gap; funding flows here are the likely policy response.
insights.quote: But screening for new crop pathogens, such as the National Plant Diagnostic Network, is minimal.
insights.locator: The United States performs disease surveillance

insights.claim: Hoard recommends adapting CDC wastewater monitoring to detect agricultural pathogens as an incremental expansion of existing infrastructure rather than a new bureaucracy.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Signals a low-cost, retrofit policy direction; favors firms in environmental/wastewater diagnostics over greenfield programs.
insights.quote: This would represent an incremental expansion of existing infrastructure, not a new bureaucracy.
insights.locator: First, we must expand pathogen surveillance

insights.claim: Funding rapid crop-pathogen field diagnostics would let county extension agents and state plant inspectors identify threats in the field rather than waiting for laboratory confirmation.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Point-of-field diagnostics is the recommended capability gap to fill; an addressable market analogous to human rapid testing.
insights.quote: the ability to identify threats in the field rather than waiting for laboratory confirmation
insights.locator: We also have to fund rapid crop pathogen detection

insights.claim: Hoard proposes a USDA rapid-response research mechanism modeled on the NSF's RAPID program to mobilize research capacity within weeks of a novel threat instead of years.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Borrowing an existing NSF model lowers the political/institutional cost of adoption; watch USDA appropriations language.
insights.quote: Modeled on the National Science Foundation's RAPID program, this would mobilize research capacity within weeks of a novel threat emerging, not years.
insights.locator: We should establish an Agriculture Department

insights.claim: Forensic attribution capability is needed because the response to a natural versus a deliberate outbreak is fundamentally different and would require USDA and CDC collaboration with State, Justice, and possibly the CIA.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Attribution turns agricultural biosecurity into a national-security/intelligence function, widening the set of agencies and budgets involved.
insights.quote: Attribution will require Agriculture Department and CDC collaboration with the departments of State and Justice and possibly the CIA.
insights.locator: And finally, we must invest in forensic capabilities

insights.claim: Hoard argues the cost-benefit math favors investment because a single novel-pathogen introduction reducing yields even fractionally would dwarf the cost of the surveillance and research infrastructure needed to prevent it.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Frames biosecurity spend as cheap insurance against a high-consequence tail event; the standard justification for funding the above programs.
insights.quote: the math cuts decisively the other way

insights.claim: Hoard frames agricultural biosecurity as the only domain of national security not yet treated with the low-probability/high-consequence insurance logic applied elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Positions food supply as an underweighted national-security category due for reclassification and investment.
insights.quote: We insure against low-probability, high-consequence events in every other domain of national security
